---
title: "2024 Learning Objectives"
---

:::{.callout-tip icon="false" collapse="false"}
### {{< fa brain >}} Learning Objectives

After completing this topic you will be able to: 


:::


## Authorship & Intellectual Credit Learning Objectives

After completing this module you will be able to:

-   <u>Define</u> types of intellectual contributions to a synthesis project
-   <u>Describe</u> some common frameworks for equitable authorship decisions
-   <u>Explain</u> benefits (or avoided costs) of making authorship decisions both collaboratively and transparently
-   <u>Create</u> a draft intellectual credit plan for your team

## Analysis & Modeling Learning Objectives

After completing this module you will be able to:

-   <u>Describe</u> proposed analytical methods to an interested audience of mixed prior experience
-   <u>Explain</u> nuance in interpretation of results of proposed analyses
-   <u>Identify</u> some statistical tests common in synthesis research
-   <u>Perform</u> some synthesis-specific analyses

## Next Steps Learning Objectives

After completing this module you will be able to:

-   <u>Identify</u> needed data and target audience(s)
-   <u>Articulate</u> connection(s) between proposed investigation and beneficial outcome
-   <u>Conceive</u> funding-worthy proposals

## Communicating Findings Learning Objectives

After completing this module you will be able to:

- <u>Define</u> elements of (in)effective communication
- <u>Identify</u> relevant audiences for a particular work
- <u>Identify</u> jargon--when to use it, when to avoid it
- <u>Translate</u> communication into different formats

## Reproducible Reports Learning Objectives

After completing this module you will be able to: 

- <u>Describe</u> contexts where computational notebooks are useful
- <u>Identify</u> the three fundamental elements of a typical notebook
- <u>Use</u> Markdown syntax to accomplish text styling
- <u>Create</u> notebooks that include a blend of plain text and embedded code chunks
- <u>Make</u> a Quarto website
- <u>Define</u> the purpose of GitHub Actions
- <u>Deploy</u> a notebook with GitHub Pages

## Spatial Data Learning Objectives

After completing this topic you will be able to: 

- <u>Define</u> the difference between the two major types of spatial data
- <u>Manipulate</u> spatial data with R
- <u>Create</u> maps using spatial data
- <u>Integrate</u> spatial data with tabular data

## Multivariate Learning Objectives

After completing this module you will be able to: 

- <u>Define</u> some common multivariate visualization methods
- <u>Describe</u> the similarities/differences between metric and non-metric ordination (methodology and interpretation)

## Interactivity Learning Objectives

After completing this topic you will be able to: 

- <u>Define</u> the three fundamental components of a Shiny app
- <u>Explain</u> benefits and limitations of interactive approaches to data exploration
- <u>Generate</u> an interactive app with Shiny
- <u>Use</u> text formatting methods in a Shiny app
- <u>Explore</u> available Shiny layout options
- <u>Create</u> a Shiny app
- <u>Describe</u> (briefly) the purpose of deploying a Shiny app
